title: Liver cancer death rate
subtitle: Reported deaths from liver [cancer](#dod:cancer) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). All deaths in a country may not have been registered with a [cause of death](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death).
caption:
Liver cancer death rate

Reported deaths from liver cancer per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 liver cancer death rates per 100,000 people, using an age-standardized metric to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. The legend ranges from 0 to over 20, with specific tick points at 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20. Mongolia stands out as the only country in the darkest red band, indicating a rate above 20 deaths per 100,000 people. Several countries in Southeast Asia, including Thailand and Vietnam, along with parts of Eastern Europe and North Africa such as Egypt, appear in the mid-range orange shades, falling between the 10 and 15 thresholds. Mexico and a few Central American nations sit in a slightly lighter orange band, near the 5 to 10 range. Most of North America, Western Europe, Australia, and South America are shaded in the lightest tones, corresponding to rates below 5. A large portion of Sub-Saharan Africa, along with Greenland and some island nations, is marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no available data. The visual pattern shows the highest concentration of liver cancer death rates in Mongolia, with moderate rates scattered across parts of Asia, Africa, and Eastern Europe, while the lowest rates are distributed across much of the Americas, Western Europe, and Oceania.
